one_line_takeaway: M3Att shows that medical multimodal RAG systems can be poisoned effectively even under a realistic query-agnostic, limited-knowledge threat model, using cluster-based proxy targets for query-agnostic image retrieval hijacking combined with clinically ambiguous (not overtly false) text edits that evade LVLM self-correction and simple corpus-filtering defenses.
key_finding: Comparing 'w/o RAG' vs 'Clean RAG' confirms retrieval normally helps LVLM performance; M3Att then substantially erodes that gain. Averaged across the reported end-to-end settings, M3Att reduces overall downstream task utility by 8.78% relative to clean RAG performance, and the paper states it outperforms the LIAR baseline across the vast majority of the 5-model x 3-retriever x multi-task grid in Table 1 (e.g., for GPT-4o+CLIP, True/False accuracy on IU-XRay drops from 89.64% clean to 77.88% under M3Att vs 83.90% under LIAR; on the Filtered subset, GPT-4o+CLIP report-generation factual consistency on MIMIC drops to 44.45% under M3Att). Retrieval-hijacking success rate (ASR@Top-k) is reported as comparable between black-box (zeroth-order) and white-box (true gradient) optimization across CLIP/BGE-VL/SigLIP and all 5 datasets, indicating the attack does not critically depend on gradient access. A case study reports Top-5 retrieval hit rate for a single poisoned image rising from about 0.01% (benign) to about 5% (after PGD optimization) without any query-distribution knowledge. Hyperparameter sweeps show retrieval ASR approaches ~100% at poison ratio 0.08, saturates with increasing PGD epsilon in the low-to-moderate regime, and plateaus with increasing cluster count K. Ablation (Fig. 4) shows both components (retrieval hijacking and text injection) are individually necessary and jointly complementary -- removing either substantially recovers downstream utility. Against three pre-retrieval filtering defenses (image clustering, text clustering, image-text consistency), retrieval ASR is reported as 'largely unchanged,' indicating the poisoned samples evade these simple distributional-anomaly defenses.
core_contribution: The paper proposes M3Att, a query-agnostic knowledge-poisoning attack framework targeting medical multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems. Unlike prior knowledge-poisoning attacks that assume the adversary knows future user queries and can optimize poisoned content per-query, M3Att assumes only limited (black-box-obtainable) knowledge of the underlying knowledge-base distribution. It combines (1) a 'distribution-guided retrieval hijacking' strategy that clusters an in-distribution reference pool via K-Means to obtain cluster-level proxy embedding targets, then uses PGD (white-box) or a zeroth-order symmetric-difference gradient estimate (black-box) to craft imperceptible, l_infinity-bounded perturbations on candidate images so they are pulled toward these proxy targets and thus retrieved for a broad range of unknown queries, with (2) a 'clinical ambiguity-guided poisoning' strategy that uses a controlled LLM editor (GPT-5) to rewrite paired medical text via three progressive perturbation layers -- fine-grained severity migration (down/up-scaling urgency), prior-constrained diagnosis distortion (swapping to a visually/probabilistically plausible alternative diagnosis rather than a random one), and risk-association corruption (urgency suppression / defensive overreach in recommendations) -- designed to degrade diagnostic accuracy while evading LVLM self-correction grounded in strong medical priors.
threat_model: Query-agnostic, weak-prior black-box/white-box poisoning of a medical multimodal RAG knowledge base. The attacker injects a limited budget of poisoned image-text entries into the retrieval corpus without access to model parameters, user queries, or retrieved contexts at inference time; the attacker is assumed to have access only to an in-distribution reference subset of the corpus (obtainable via black-box interaction with the RAG system) used to profile cluster structure. Both white-box (direct gradient access to the image encoder) and black-box (zeroth-order gradient estimation via symmetric finite differences) variants of the retrieval-hijacking optimization are evaluated. The goal is to maximize retrieval likelihood of poisoned entries for arbitrary/unknown future queries and to mislead the downstream LVLM into clinically incorrect but plausible outputs.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitation is that validation is restricted to representative 2D medical imaging modalities (chest X-rays, histopathology patches); 3D volumetric modalities (CT, MRI) and temporal medical video are not empirically evaluated, though the authors claim the framework is 'readily extensible.' Additional limitations evident from the text but not explicitly flagged by the authors: the only external baseline is a single text-only general-domain attack (LIAR); no comparison is made against other recent multimodal-RAG poisoning attacks the paper itself cites (e.g., MM-PoisonRAG, Poisoned-MRAG, HV-Attack) despite discussing them in Related Work. The text-poisoning component relies on GPT-5 as an uncontrolled-content 'editor,' which is itself a commercial model whose future availability/behavior could change reproducibility. The reported 8.78% average utility degradation is an aggregate figure whose exact computation basis (which subset of Table 1 cells, which retrievers/models included) is not fully specified in the paper.

quantitative_results: Average downstream task utility reduction of 8.78% vs. clean RAG across evaluated settings
Case study: Top-5 retrieval hit rate for a poisoned sample rises from ~0.01% (before PGD optimization) to ~5% (after)
Retrieval ASR approaches nearly 100% at poison ratio 0.08 (Fig. 3)
GPT-4o + CLIP retriever, IU-XRay True/False accuracy: 89.64% (Clean RAG avg.) vs 83.90% (LIAR) vs 77.88% (M3Att)
GPT-4o + CLIP, Filtered subset, MIMIC report-generation factual consistency (FC): 58.24% (LIAR) vs 55.56% (M3Att); completeness (CP): 46.53% (LIAR) vs 44.45% (M3Att)
GPT-4o + CLIP, PCam image classification accuracy: 66.62% (Clean RAG avg.) vs 71.50% (LIAR) vs 50.02% (M3Att)
Poison budget equals the number of clusters K=40, yielding a poison rate below 0.01 in all settings
PGD settings: epsilon=16/255, alpha=1/255, N=500 iterations, 10-iteration candidate warm-up
baselines_compared: LIAR (Tan et al. 2024) -- text-only general-domain RAG poisoning attack, adapted as the closest query-agnostic-compatible prior method
Clean RAG (unpoisoned retrieval, averaged across retrievers)
